speaker: interviewer
text: Tape three. The survivor is Susan Brunn. Let's pick up on having you tell us about when you got pregnant.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I got pregnant, it was no time to be pregnant. And we tried to eliminate my pregnancy, which somehow didn't work. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we took a family doctor-- the family doctor off my husband's family into-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we-- we talked to him. He was very, very anti-Nazi always. And it was a Dr. Koenig. And he examined me. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he said he would come to the house and he would help me deliver the baby. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As it turned out, about a few months, three months or so before-- I don't know exactly how long before. He said to me it will not be possible. , The baby's not lying in a perfect position. And will be all kinds-- and I have to try to go into a hospital. And there was a route I  remember to go to Yugoslavia to one of the other countries. And they wanted to get me out of Germany for the duration of the birth. And-- but-- also that didn't work out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my husband had the bright idea to talk to a friend of his, who was also like him, a Mischling, and try to get his sister to give me her papers that I could go under her name to a hospital because she was legal. She was married to another Mischling, and they lived legal.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as  he talked to him, it turned out that he said, no. It can't be done. Because his sister just had a baby. And she can't have three months later another one. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So this friend of his-- his name was Hans-- had a girlfriend, very nice girl, who was also very, very anti-Nazi. And he told her the story and took a chance. And this girl was happy to do something against Hitler. And she came with him to the place where we were living-- where my husband and I were living-- and offered her papers and her help to me. And my mother was there. I remember that. And she said to her, do you know what you're doing? Because you can get really into deep water with it, and when you have now a child out of wedlock. Later on, you meet somebody who wants to marry you, and this is always in your papers that you had a child.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, I don't care. If he really loves me, I will explain the story to him. And then he will either marry me or not marry me. I want to do it. And she helped me. She gave me her papers. And we became the best of friends. And I went to the hospital under her name and gave birth under her name. And that's how we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hella Moebes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hella is H-E-L-L-A. And her second name, Moebes, is M-O-E-B-E-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your child's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was at that time, she was born under the name Hannelore Moebes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the hospital that you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very nice hospital in-- in Dahlem-- Berlin Dahlem. It was called Waldfrieden.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a delivery did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: A very bad one. I was in labor for close to two days. And then I had to get an injection to speed up the delivery. And then I didn't have the  afterbirth. The placenta was-- I had to be operated. And I was quite sick. And the child was very little and very-- I had not very much to eat, so no-- the care wasn't there, the milk, and whatever you had to have. She was like five pounds. So it was not incubated. But it was very little. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I found even a picture how she looked. Big head and very small body. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And well-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] unfortunately, I never found Hella anymore. After, of course, by the end of the war, we were still OK. But I-- after the war, she-- she disappeared. She went-- she married a Dutchman and went with him. I don't even know the name. And she went to Holland to live with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you take the baby to live?

speaker: interviewee
text: We took the baby home for one day. And then we took her to a Catholic home-- to a-- what do you call that-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] where they had children with no parents or only one parent, and-- or where they wanted to get them out of Berlin, because Berlin was bombed so badly. And she was brought there under the name Hannelore Moebes. And I was a single mother who brought her there. And my mother came with me. She was the aunt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they were Catholic sisters who run that home. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's where she was brought to.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did she stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was brought there in January of 1944. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we picked her up in May, '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often do you go to see her?

speaker: interviewee
text: As long as we could every week until Hella was caught through something else, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and it wasn't safe anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Klaus go with you to visit the baby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Klaus was my cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did the money come from for the two of you to live in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Klaus.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Klaus was wealthy. Klaus had real estate in Berlin, big apartment buildings. And the income alone from the buildings carried-- carried us very nicely.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your life like besides that with Klaus at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was in hiding with him. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] financially, I had no worries. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He got-- on the black market he got food. Everything was available on the black market. I mean, it was harder times. You had to know where to go. But he got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what kind of things did he get?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything. Meat, poultry, butter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you share that with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a life did you have with him socially at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no social life. There were-- we-- we were glad if we could be alone and left alone, and afraid to go out too much. So we went once in a blue moon to  a movie at night. But that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a place did you  live in?

speaker: interviewee
text: He rented a very nice apartment under his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, god. I knew it the other day. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Ansbacher Strasse in-- Ansbacher Strasse.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ansbacher Strasse [GERMAN], 56.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in Berlin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah. Also in a quite nice neighborhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any friends at that time in the neighborhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. His friend, Han Hagen. And I had another friend who was a Mischling. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And that was it. We didn't keep much. You tried to stay away from everybody as much as possible. And even your best friend, you wouldn't say where you lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: You were afraid if they get caught, and they were put to the question, whom else can you bring in? Then we let you stay here for another six months. For each person you bring in, you-- you get a certain period for yourself that you don't get transported to Auschwitz or wherever. So then they would know where we are, and they could finger us. Didn't tell anybody where you lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the evidences of war in Berlin at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there  were bombings almost weekly. Daily, at times. And I loved it. It was always a lot of confusion. And everybody was much-- it was easier to get around, because everybody was confused. The officers were not as strict with everything. There were times at that time that I went to a bombed out house. They-- let's say there was a bombing, an air raid. And the house was bombed during the night. And in the morning, they had near that house set up a station for people who lived there to come and get-- they lost everything-- to get new ration cards. And of course, I took my luck in my hands that somebody might say, oh, she's not in our house. But they were all so preoccupied with themselves, they didn't pay any mind to anybody else. Everybody was standing in line and got a ration card. And so I got a ration card too.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes, not always.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many ration cards were you able to collect?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't know. Maybe once in a  while. Not always. But I was lucky at some times to be able to. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So that helped us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you knew that war was coming to an end? Where were you? And what was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where were we? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We were at a point-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] '44 in  January. Where-- I think we were [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] back in his mother's place-- in Klaus's mother's place. Because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he had to give up the apartment. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Oh, that was the time when he had to go to Erfurt. And I went with him for a couple of months. And then I went to his mother's place with my mother together. We didn't-- she didn't have a place anymore and I didn't have a place anymore. And she said, you come here. She lived outside of Berlin in a very beautiful neighborhood in a big house. I mean, much bigger than mine, or-- even though it was only the small house from the bad times. And not many people around. So we stayed there for quite a while. And that's where we got caught.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody must have fingered us. And we were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by two agents. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One day they came and just blocked the back door and the front door, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] picked us up. Wanted to pick us up. And I was able through some kind of trick to get away, hoping that they would run after me and look for me. And in the meantime, my mother could be hidden by my mother-in-law. And it was like that. But my mother was breathing very heavy because she had asthma already at that time. And so they found her breathing behind the [NON-ENGLISH] in the basement. And they took her. And if I wouldn't have had a child, I would have maybe gone voluntary too. But my first consideration was to try to live for the baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your mother go?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was put to Oranienburger Strasse where the big temple is. That's where they had the headquarter. And everybody was brought there. And it was already in October, 1944. So Auschwitz was already, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think, partly under siege. The Russians were near already, I think. And they sent them to camps within Germany. She was brought to Ravensbruck. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And we stayed even in contact. She was allowed to get one letter a month. And she was allowed to write one letter a month. And she wrote to her sister-in-law, to my Tante Maria. And-- and I wrote to her as if I'm Tante Maria, but with my handwriting so she knew that I'm all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened to me is [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had to disappear, of course, from my mother-in-law's house. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I went-- no. When I ran away, I went to the basement. I asked for-- for permission to put the stuff which I was doing into the place in the basement where she kept her groceries and all. And they let me, because two doors were under control, not knowing that there was a third door from the basement going out into the garden. And that's where I escaped from. And instead of running out in the street and running away, I was in the next building in the bushes hiding for a day and a half until everything was clear. They never went to that part, because it was a headquarters of the Gestapo. They took the big house from the Wertheims, which had, I think, 63 rooms, and made it in a headquarters. And the little house was next to it. That was a former gardener's house. And so I was hiding there until the coast was clear. And then I ran to friends of my mother-in-law who knew about me. And they helped me from then on.

speaker: interviewer
text: The friends of your mother-in-law-- what religion were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Gentile. They were also Protestant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Erx Leben. Mr. and Mrs. Erx Leben. E-R-X L-E-B-E-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after you left their house, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: To another Gentile family who knew me before the war. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And from there on to another place after two weeks, three weeks, four weeks. We always shifted around.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you next meet Klaus?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we met-- we met more or less all the time. He came to where I was. They knew about him. Or we met at nighttime somewhere on the corner of so and so and went for a walk. Or we-- we met all the time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And about-- and the fighting was directly in Berlin. He said to me, enough is enough. And we are going out to our country house in Glienicke, because I'm sure that the person who was living in that house has fled. And we're going to check it out. And he was in touch with the neighbor there to find out. And he found out that they had left. They left the old mother and the maid. But the husband and wife had disappeared. He was  a high officer in the-- in the SS or in some-- some kind of an organization. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so he went with me. And we took along [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a maid, which was many years ago working for us-- for them, for us. And she was a very good person. And she knew all-- all those years about me. I was in her house too for a couple of weeks, even though she had only a one-room apartment. And we took her along outside of Berlin too, because we didn't want her in-- in the bombings and the fighting. There were street fights. It was like-- I can't explain it. It was like every house when you see in the movies today in-- in Korea or where it was. Every house had to be taken. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was fighting with who?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians with the Germans. The German soldiers didn't want to give up or were forced not to give up. And you could see the most horrible things in the street. There were some German soldiers who didn't want to fight anymore. And they deserted. And they strung them up on the-- on a lamppost. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did that upset you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wouldn't that upset anybody? I don't know. It upset me a lot. And we went out of Berlin and-- to his house. And as we drove out, the first American soldiers entered the city. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was-- that was the moment I can never, never, never forget and describe to anybody. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe it to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: The feeling? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, we are liberated. And we saw the Americans coming in. They came in from the west while the Russians came from the east. And we had to go out to the west, to the house. And that guy must have thought I'm a nut, because I ran over to them, and I hugged them and I kissed them and I screamed. And I cried. And that was it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we realized that we were saved. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That feeling, you cannot describe. You can't. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't think anybody could.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got to Glienicke, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was a housewife in Glienicke after the war was over. I didn't do anything. In fact, I lived quite comfortable. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My husband got me  a housekeeper. And after we found my mother, and the baby, and we were all united, we had quite a comfortable life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we had very nice American and British friends. We had a neighbor of my husband who we knew as a little boy who came back with the English army as a soldier. And through them, we met a lot of people. And we were invited to American parties and to British parties. And we had a very nice life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to this country in '47. In March of '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you decide to leave Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I didn't-- I-- I couldn't look at the Germans. I just wanted to get out. I didn't feel happy there, and I wanted to get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did Klaus feel about leaving Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same way. He also felt-- he was a little more hesitant because his roots were there and the-- he hoped all his property and-- but to this day, he said to me, I'm glad that you made me come over here. I mean, to this day. I haven't spoken to him in years, in the last two years maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do for a living while you were still there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. In the end after the war, he didn't work for a  while. He-- he had enough income from his property.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to-- specifically when and where did you arrive in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived here on March 13, I think. March 13 or 14, 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do when you arrived here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what did I do? We were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] put into the-- we came through the HIAS. And we were put into the building of the HIAS in-- on Lafayette Street. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it didn't take long. Took about a week until we found an apartment together with another couple who was on the boat. A terrible apartment, but we took it. [LAUGHS] It was in the Bronx on Jerome-- off Jerome Avenue on the fifth floor walk up. But it was ours. It was-- We. Shared it. We couldn't afford the rent alone. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he looked for a job, and I looked for a job. And I had a job within three days. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Also through a friend of mine who came here with a couple of boats before me. And she told me what to do and what not to do. And I worked in the garment center. I was working for Peter Pan Brassieres, piecework. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he found a job as a machinist somewhere. Unfortunately, he couldn't ever hold a job. He changed one year, I think, seven or eight times. He was a very good worker, but very, very slow. And this is not for America. You've got to be fast here. I don't know why, but you have to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think he left Germany and gave up that kind of an income to come here and struggle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, firstly, he didn't-- at that point, he didn't want to be separated from me. And I said, I don't want to stay here. And secondly, he-- he thought the income is there, and they can send it over all the time, which was possible for a while. We did. But then when Berlin was divided, all his property was in the Russian zone. And it-- it was taken away from him. In the meantime, he got it back when the war was broken down. But for years, he didn't get anything. But he wouldn't have gotten anything either if he would have stayed over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll pick up on the next tape. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
